Version 2.0 | April 2026 | Confidential Azad Karoar is a transformative community-driven initiative established in 2019 in the Sargodha District of Punjab, Pakistan. The organization was founded with the vision of empowering local entrepreneurs, artisans, and small business owners by providing them with the tools, knowledge, and networks necessary to thrive in modern competitive markets. This document serves as the central knowledge repository for the Azad Karoar project, containing organizational history, operational frameworks, financial data, program descriptions, impact assessments, and strategic roadmaps.
heading: Comprehensive Project Report & Knowledge Base

heading: Table of Contents

text: Azad Karoar (Urdu: IIIIIIIIIII), meaning "Free Enterprise" in English, was conceived during a series of community consultations held in Sargodha city between January and March 2019. The founders — a group of local businesspeople, retired civil servants, and development professionals — identified a critical gap: thousands of informal workers and micro-entrepreneurs in the region possessed genuine skills and market opportunities but lacked access to formal financial services, business training, and supply chain networks. The organization was formally registered under the Societies Registration Act of 1860 with the Government of Punjab on 15 August 2019. Initial seed funding of PKR 4.5 million was raised from 12 founding members and three local philanthropists. By the end of 2019, Azad Karoar had enrolled 120 beneficiaries across its first pilot programs in three union councils of Sargodha District.
heading: 1. Organization Overview & History

heading: 1.1 Founding Timeline

text: To empower economically marginalized individuals and micro-entrepreneurs in rural and peri-urban Punjab by providing access to financial services, vocational training, digital tools, and market linkages — enabling them to build sustainable, dignified livelihoods and contribute to inclusive local economic growth.
heading: 2.1 Mission Statement

text: A Punjab where every person, regardless of gender, class, or geography, has the freedom and capability to pursue meaningful enterprise and achieve economic independence.
heading: 2.2 Vision Statement

heading: 2.3 Core Values

text: Azad Karoar operates seven flagship programs, each targeting a distinct dimension of economic empowerment. Programs are designed to be complementary — beneficiaries are encouraged to participate in multiple programs to maximize impact.
heading: 3. Programs & Initiatives

text: The flagship vocational training program offering 3–6 month certified courses in 14 trades including tailoring, embroidery, mobile phone repair, solar panel installation, plumbing, electrical work, food processing, and livestock management. As of April 2026, KSP has trained 3,420 individuals with an 82% employment or self-employment rate within six months of graduation. Key KSP Statistics (2019–2026): • Total graduates: 3,420 • Female graduates: 1,890 (55.3%) • Average course duration: 4.2 months • Placement rate (employment/self-employment): 82% • Average income increase post-training: PKR 8,400/month • Training centers: 11 across 6 districts • Accredited by Technical Education & Vocational Training Authority (TEVTA)
heading: 3.1 Karobar Skills Program (KSP)

text: The Micro-Finance Window provides interest-free qarz-e-hasna (benevolent loans) and subsidized credit facilities to micro-entrepreneurs. Loans range from PKR 25,000 to PKR 500,000 with flexible repayment schedules of 12–36 months. The program operates under partnership with three registered microfinance institutions and the Akhuwat Foundation.
heading: 3.2 Micro-Finance Window (MFW)

text: Launched in July 2021, the Azad Digital Program addresses the digital divide by providing free training in smartphone literacy, e-commerce, digital marketing, freelancing platforms (Upwork, Fiverr), online banking, and basic cybersecurity. The program operates through 8 computer labs and a mobile training van that visits remote union councils. The ADP has trained 2,100 individuals to date, with 340 participants now earning income through freelancing platforms. Monthly collective earnings of ADP freelancers reached PKR 6.2 million in March 2026, with average individual earnings of PKR 18,200/month.
heading: 3.3 Azad Digital Program (ADP)

text: The WEA is a specialized 6-month accelerator for women entrepreneurs launched in January 2024. It combines business skills training, mentorship, market access support, and a PKR 100,000–300,000 seed grant. Two cohorts have been completed with 89 graduates. A third cohort of 45 women is currently in progress. Sectors include home-based food businesses, handicrafts, beauty services, and agri-processing.
heading: 3.4 Women Entrepreneurs Accelerator (WEA)

text: AgriBoost targets small-scale farmers and livestock owners in peri-urban and rural areas of the six districts. Services include: soil testing, modern farming technique workshops, access to quality seeds and fertilizers through bulk purchasing groups, livestock vaccination camps, and linkages with Agricultural Development Bank of Pakistan (ADBP) for seasonal credit. 890 farming households are currently enrolled.
heading: 3.5 AgriBoost Initiative

text: YEP focuses on youth aged 18–30 who are neither in employment, education, nor training (NEET). The 4-month program combines soft skills development, entrepreneurship training, and a structured business plan competition with prizes of PKR 50,000–200,000. Since 2022, YEP has enrolled 780 youth with 65% reporting positive livelihood outcomes.
heading: 3.6 Youth Enterprise Program (YEP)

text:
Launched in April 2024, KarobarHub (www.karobarhub.pk) is a B2C and B2B e-commerce platform connecting Azad Karoar beneficiaries with buyers across Pakistan. The platform lists handcrafted goods, agri-products, food items, and services. As of April 2026, it has 620 active seller accounts, 8,400 product listings, and has processed PKR 14.2 million in transactions.

text:
Azad Karoar is governed by a 9-member Board of Directors elected by the General Body. Day-to-day operations are managed by a professional Executive Team headquartered in Sargodha with field offices in six districts. Total staff strength: 68 full-time employees, 22 part-time trainers/consultants.

heading: 4. Organizational Structure

text:
Azad Karoar operates on a transparent, donor-funded model with strict compliance to SECP and FBR regulations. All accounts are audited annually by an independent CA firm. Below is a summary of financial performance for the fiscal years 2022–2026. Note: PKR = Pakistani Rupees. FY 2025-26 figures are budgeted projections.

text: Azad Karoar's Monitoring, Evaluation and Learning (MEL) system tracks beneficiary outcomes through baseline surveys, quarterly check-ins, and annual impact assessments. All data is stored in the organization's custom-built HMIS (Household Management Information System).

heading: 8. Partnerships & Stakeholders

text: Azad Karoar has invested significantly in building a robust technology backbone to support program delivery, data management, and beneficiary services.
heading: 9. Technology & Digital Infrastructure

text: The Board of Directors approved the 5-year Strategic Plan 2026–2030 in December 2025. The plan is organized around four strategic pillars aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs 1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 17).
heading: 10. 2026–2030 Strategic Plan

text: Target: Reach 25,000 active beneficiaries by 2030 across 12 districts of Punjab. • Expand to 6 new districts: Jhang, Faisalabad Rural, Gujranwala Rural, Muzaffargarh, Layyah, Dera Ghazi Khan • Open 8 new training centers with TEVTA accreditation • Increase female beneficiary share to 65% • Establish a dedicated PWD (Persons with Disabilities) program • Develop a rural-to-urban migration support program
heading: Strategic Pillar 1: Scale & Deepen Impact

text: Target: Achieve 40% self-generated revenue by 2030, reducing donor dependency. • Scale KarobarHub to PKR 200 million annual GMV by 2028 • Launch a paid premium training certification program • Establish an Azad Karoar Social Enterprise Fund • Introduce training fees on a sliding-scale basis for higher-income participants • Seek SECP registration as a Social Enterprise by 2027
heading: Strategic Pillar 2: Financial Sustainability

text: Target: Build an AI-powered beneficiary support system and fully digital program delivery. • Launch Azad AI — a WhatsApp chatbot providing business advisory in Urdu • Integrate KarobarHub with Daraz, Alibaba.com, and regional B2B platforms • Deploy IoT-based soil and water sensors for AgriBoost enrolled farmers • Build a real-time dashboard for donors and government stakeholders • Develop a mobile app for beneficiaries to access all services
heading: Strategic Pillar 3: Technology & Innovation

text: Target: Contribute to at least 3 provincial policy reforms benefiting informal workers by 2030. • Launch a Punjab Informal Workers Advocacy Network with 20 partner organizations • Publish annual State of Micro-Enterprise report for Punjab • Engage National Assembly and Punjab Assembly standing committees on economic policy • Submit shadow reports to UN Human Rights Council on economic rights • Partner with BISP and Ehsaas Program for beneficiary referral integration
heading: Strategic Pillar 4: Advocacy & Policy Influence

text: Rukhsana Bibi, 34, from Union Council 7 of Sargodha District, enrolled in the Karobar Skills Program in February 2022 for a tailoring and embroidery course. A mother of three with no prior formal income, Rukhsana had learned basic stitching from her mother but lacked the skills to produce market-quality garments. After completing her 5-month certification, Rukhsana accessed a PKR 80,000 loan from the Micro-Finance Window and purchased an industrial sewing machine and overlocker. She also joined the Azad Digital Program and learned to market her products on Facebook and Instagram. By December 2022, Rukhsana was earning PKR 22,000/month — a household income increase of over 300%. By April 2026, Rukhsana employs 4 women from her neighborhood, has a dedicated KarobarHub storefront with PKR 1.2 million in cumulative sales, and was selected as a WEA cohort 2 mentor. She credits Azad Karoar's integrated approach: 'It was not just one thing. The training, the loan, the digital skills — all together changed everything.'
heading: Case Study 1: Rukhsana Bibi — From Home to Enterprise

text: Muhammad Aamir, 23, from Bhakkar District, enrolled in the Youth Enterprise Program in June 2023. Having dropped out of intermediate college due to financial pressure, Aamir had been working irregular daily-wage construction jobs. He was referred to Azad Karoar by a local government school teacher. Aamir showed exceptional aptitude in the digital skills modules and was fast-tracked into the Azad Digital Program's freelancing track. Within 6 months of completing training, he had created profiles on Upwork and Fiverr, initially offering data entry services. He won his first contract — $35 for a data cleaning task — in November 2023. By April 2026, Aamir earns an average of $350–500/month (PKR 97,000–139,000) through freelance data analysis and Excel automation services. He has completed over 120 contracts with a 98% client satisfaction score. He now volunteers as a weekend trainer at the Bhakkar digital center, having trained 40 new freelancers.
heading: Case Study 2: Muhammad Aamir — From NEET Youth to Tech Freelancer

text:
In Mianwali District, 23 small-scale wheat and cotton farmers with an average landholding of 2.5 acres each faced persistent challenges: expensive inputs, exploitative middlemen, and lack of market information. They enrolled in the AgriBoost Initiative in early 2024. Through bulk purchasing facilitated by Azad Karoar, input costs fell by 18%. Soil testing revealed deficiencies that, once corrected with targeted fertilizer, increased wheat yield by 22%. The group was also connected to a flour mill in Sargodha that agreed to purchase their produce at 8% above market rate in exchange for quality consistency guaranteed by Azad Karoar's output quality monitoring. Net income per farmer increased by PKR 35,000 per season. The group has since formalized as a registered Farmer Producer Organization (FPO) and is exploring wheat flour packaging under a shared brand for direct retail sales.

heading: 12. Challenges & Risk Register

text: Azad Karoar maintains full compliance with all applicable Pakistani laws and international donor standards. Key governance documents include the Constitution, Financial Management Policy, HR Policy, Anti-Corruption & Whistleblowing Policy, Child Protection Policy, Gender Equality Policy, and Data Protection Policy.
heading: 13. Governance & Compliance
